## [00:00 - 03:03]

In this lesson, we're going to cover how to use Nano and Micro Influencers to generate authentic platform native content at scale, to quickly grow your brand's social following, and to run profitable campaigns that attract with discount codes. And all of this will of course be at a fraction of the cost of macro influencers, or even just in return for being gifted your product. As we identified last lesson, Nano Influencers have anywhere between 1,000 to 10,000 followers, and Micro Influencers have 10,000 to 50,000 followers. The main upside to using Nano or Micro Influencers is that they'll often work solely in return for being gifted your product, or if your product retails at a lower price, say under the $50 mark, then you might offer a small payment for the collaboration as well. We've found that by offering small payments, or vouchers, to the price of $25 to $50, helps to significantly raise the campaign completion rate, which is the main issue with Micro Influencer campaigns across the board. In unpaid product-only campaigns, the influencer won't always post because they're not necessarily being paid to do so. So this is not a binding contract. It's similar to NPR, where you send out hundreds of press releases and not every media publication picks them up. This is why it's often called product seeding, as not every seed you plant will grow. A lot of brands still think of gifting your product as a transaction, which is more so the case with higher ticket items that retail in the hundreds of dollars. However, the best attitude here will be just that you win some and you lose some, and as long as you aim for around a 50% completion rate, you'll still get good results from a campaign like this. What you need to work out when scaling your Micro Influencer outreach is at what point you break even. So what the price is to send the product out to each influencer in terms of the management time, the product cost, and the shipping. Once you know this, you're able to better understand how many influencers you need to have post and make a sale when it comes to scaling your Micro Influencer sendouts. Another reference I sometimes draw on here is that of venture capitalists. They only need around one out of ten investments to work out, as the payoff will often be over ten times their initial investment. So this is another way to think of your Nano and Micro Influencer work when it's only in return for product. Plus, outside of direct sales, you'll also have a constant stream of content and reviews to share to your social channels, as well as an uplift in direct and social traffic and social media followers to convert into your customers. One of the best ways to increase your campaign completion rate will be to build a strong relationship with your influencers, which we'll be covering in a later lesson, as well as following up with influencers. Another great way to increase the campaign completion rate is to offer a small payment

## [03:03 - 06:03]

or voucher with the product. For example, for Drop Bottle, which is my fruit-infuser drink bottle brand, if we want the influencer to buy some beautiful fruit to infuse into their bottle, we might give them a $10 spending money for the fruit so that way they're not out of pocket. One of our clients, the Ooty, which is an oversized robe crossed with a hoodie, will offering YouTubers the product plus a $25 Uber Eats voucher to do a mukbang video, which is a video where they pretty much just sit there and eat a whole lot of food while interacting with their audience, and they're a bit of a trend at the moment. A good way to do this is to offer the additional payment once they've posted, because once you're sending out the product only, you've already fulfilled your end of the deal. And by sending the product, there's nothing left that you have on your end to motivate the influencer to post outside of your words. So this can be a really strong incentive and will work especially well if your product has a relatively low retail price. One of the downsides of working with nano and micro influencers is that managing them at scale can be quite time consuming, but I'm going to share my exact workflow for doing this with you in the next lesson on scaling micro influencers. So let's start off with our first type of campaign, which will be getting influencers to generate content on your brand's behalf. Usually simply in return for your product, therefore you probably won't be surprised that this is often referred to as a product for content campaign. A product for content campaign is very similar to a product for post campaign. However, rather than posting the content to their social channels, the influencer simply submits the content directly to your brand. A product for content campaign is a great way to create influencer generated content at scale in a cost effective way. We all know that our brands need a constant stream of content, whether it's to post to our social channels, to use for paid advertising, or on our websites as social proof. The problem is that branded content can be really expensive to create with professional photographers, studio fees, models, lighting, editing, the list goes on. And even then, branded content doesn't perform as well as influencer generated content on social media. This is because consumers are increasingly tuning out branded content. Think about it, when you're scrolling through your Instagram feed and you see a stock image or something that looks like avert advertising, what do you do? Most of the time you keep scrolling. Influencer generated content on the other hand is authentic platform native content that audiences can both identify with and engage with. Influencer generated content is the way forward for brands and a product for content campaign is a great way to scale it. So whereas in a product for post campaign, you might get one piece of branded content, in a product for content campaign, you might be able to ask for a little more content. This will depend on the relative cost of your product. If your product's worth less than $50, for example, I wouldn't ask for much more than a couple of pieces of content. But if it's over $100, you should be able to get away with asking for two to three pieces of static content and maybe even a video to be created, for example.

## [06:03 - 09:06]

You might ask the influencer for the content to be filtered or edited in the same way that they'd usually edit a photo before uploading it to their social channels. Yes, this method takes a social side out of influencer marketing entirely, but it leads to more content assets being created and a higher campaign completion rate than a product for post campaign. In terms of a campaign completion rate, as we discussed earlier, influencers won't always post when it comes to unpaid gifting campaigns. From my experience with other brands, the post completion rate for a new brand is usually closer to 50% to 60%, and for a product for content campaign, this figure's usually closer to the 90% rate. Even though posting the content to social isn't a mandatory in this campaign, we've found that if influencers really like the content they've created for your brand, they'll end up posting it at some point anyway. So really, it's a win-win. We had a really strong product for content campaign going on in the early days of the fifth, to the extent that we even had some of our top content creators on paid monthly content retainers. To this day, if you check out the fifth's Instagram feed, it's beautifully curated with a consistent aesthetic and a blend of both brand and influencer-generated content using this campaign method. Recently, we ran a huge product for content campaign on behalf of a brand I advise, who's also a client of Hayes called Calming Blankets. The influencer brief went a little something like, we're looking for our weighted blankets to be trialled by influencers on Instagram and YouTube via a candid slash natural video. You'll describe the benefits of the weighted blanket. Furthermore, mention how it's helped with your stress, insomnia or anxiety. We can create your unique code to allow us to do future collaborations. Any content created will need to be sent directly to us as we may decide to repost. In terms of the deliverables of this campaign, we were asking for a 30 to 60 second video, as described in the brief, and three images, both in their edited and non-edited form. This was in return for a blanket valued at $299 and a silk I'm asked valued at $50, so quite high ticket items. We're about to add an extra deliverable to this campaign as well. Once the influencers submitted their content and testimonial to us, we're going to get them to leave it as a testimonial on Calming Blankets website, Facebook page or even directly onto one of their Facebook ads. As we all know, reviews are a really important form of social proof, so why not involve your influencers in this process too? Imagine this at scale monthly with hundreds of positive reviews straight onto your top performing Facebook ads and website. So finally, in terms of tracking content, it's useful to ask the influencer to submit the content directly to your brand via email or by creating them a Dropbox or Google Drive folder to upload the content to. A quick little extra tip in regard to the video testimonial that you're getting is using this formula. So you want to start with their reaction to your product as a hook, then get them to highlight their problem they had before your product, the way it affected them and why

## [09:06 - 09:39]

everyone should be aware of this problem. Then you want to get them to move on to how your product is the perfect solution to this problem, get them to demonstrate how to use the product, its UVPs and benefits, and then in results guaranteeing that the product works and is worthwhile purchasing. Finally you want to add in any offer or call to action that you'd like them to highlight. It could even be a good idea to write out a sample script for them to make it simpler, but make sure that you say that you'd love it to be in their own words and from their own experiences.
